title: Why Your Margin Falls in a Quiet Month Even Though Nothing Went Wrong
linkedin:
Every job priced correctly. Gross margin holding steady on paper. And profit still fell this month.

We see this pattern a lot in the weeks after EOFY, and it rarely gets named because nothing on the invoice level looks wrong.

Here's the mechanism. Rent, insurance, loan repayments and salaried wages don't shrink when trading softens for a few weeks. They stay fixed. So when volume dips even slightly, that same fixed cost pool gets spread across fewer sales, and each sale ends up carrying a heavier share of it than it did last month.

A professional services firm feels this through utilisation, the same wage bill against fewer billable hours. A trade business feels it through plant and licensing costs that keep running between jobs. A cafe feels it through rent and rostered wages that don't shrink just because foot traffic has.

Checking the margin on individual jobs won't catch this. It's a business-wide calculation, not a per-job one. The businesses that move through a quiet patch calmly are usually the ones that already know their break-even point, the exact volume needed each month before fixed costs are covered.

Without that number, a soft month reads as vague unease. With it, you can say precisely how much headroom exists before it becomes a genuine concern.

A quiet month after a busy EOFY period isn't a verdict on the business. It's a maths problem with a known shape.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Ever had a month where every job was priced right, nothing went wrong, and profit still came in lower than expected?

It usually isn't a pricing problem. Rent, insurance, loan repayments and wages don't ease off just because trading has gone a bit quiet for a few weeks. So the same fixed costs end up spread across fewer sales, and each one quietly carries more weight than it did last month.

It's one of the most common patterns we see across owner-led businesses right after the EOFY rush, and once it's named, most owners recognise it straight away from a quiet patch they couldn't quite explain at the time.

Has your business ever had a "good month on paper" that didn't feel like one in the bank account?

#Profitability
google:
A note for East Coast business owners heading into a quieter stretch of winter trading: if a slow month has you double checking your pricing, the issue might not be your prices at all.

Costs like rent, insurance, loan repayments and salaried wages stay fixed even when trading softens for a few weeks after the EOFY rush. So the same cost base ends up spread across fewer sales, and profit can fall even though every job or invoice is priced exactly as it should be. Checking the margin on a handful of recent jobs won't show this, because it's a whole-of-business calculation, not a per-job one.

The businesses that ride out a quiet patch calmly are usually the ones that already know their break-even point, the exact level of sales needed each month before fixed costs are covered. That number turns a vague worry into something specific and measurable.

If July or August has felt quieter than expected despite solid work going out the door, it's worth understanding whether this pattern is behind it before assuming something has gone wrong. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
